doom.driver: Ebola outbreak now third largest recorded and "spreading rapidly". Ebola outbreak risk level increased as deaths reach 177 with nearly 750 cases.
doom.blurb: EBOLA GOES FROM BAD TO THIRD-WORST ON RECORD
doom.summary: The DRC Ebola outbreak has tripled in a week, hit 177 dead, and WHO has raised risk to 'very high' — all while aid cuts and community distrust are actively hamstringing the response. It's the third-largest outbreak ever recorded, which is the kind of superlative nobody asked for. Meanwhile, an experimental mRNA vaccine exists but isn't deployed, Russian satellites are stalking radar infrastructure in orbit, and Putin is threatening retaliation over Ukraine strikes — so today's doom is a genuinely uncomfortable multi-front situation.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Trump abruptly cancelled an AI safety EO signing after top CEOs declined to attend, signalling governance is fragmenting even as capabilities race forward.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Tulsi Gabbard resigning as US national intelligence director mid-conflict creates a notable leadership vacuum at a genuinely dangerous moment.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Putin vows retaliation after Ukraine strikes Moscow-occupied region; US munitions analysts warn stockpiles are actively shaping war decisions.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Anthropic's Project Glasswing initial update dropped quietly; no immediate red flags, but the transparency is notable given the capability trajectory.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Ebola outbreak now third-largest on record with 750 cases, tripling in a week, in a context of gutted international aid and active community distrust — a structural containment failure in progress.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — interior scene, camera positioned inside a dim Ugandan border-town family dwelling in Kasese district, looking outward through a low rectangular window cut into a cinderblock wall, the window frame dividing the composition between the dark suffocating interior and the wet world outside, inside foreground dominated by a wooden table holding a single kerosene lamp casting deep crimson-amber pools of light across a crumpled contact tracing form, ink bleeding where rainwater has soaked the paper, a hand-painted "Centre de Santé" directional sign leaning against the interior wall its letters half-legible, outside through the window a red laterite dirt road glistens under heavy equatorial rain, a white Toyota Land Cruiser caked in mud to its door handles sits idling at the road's edge its headlights cutting pale cones through diagonal rain sheets, a figure in a yellow Tyvek coverall visible through the vehicle's fogged passenger window a face shield catching the lamplight, dense dark jungle wall pressing in from the road's far edge blurring into charcoal under low rainclouds, everything outside rendered in deep grey-green shadow and wet black, the window frame acting as a threshold between domestic warmth and the viral dark beyond, color palette of dark crimson interior bleeding into charcoal and wet slate outside, high contrast oppressive.

dawn.driver: SpaceX launches its biggest, most beefed-up Starship yet on a test flight. The mega rocket made its debut two days after SpaceX CEO Elon Musk announced he's taking the company public. NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman said Starship is now one step closer to the moon.
dawn.blurb: HUMANITY'S BIGGEST ROCKET CLEARS THE TOWER
dawn.summary: Against a backdrop of mine disasters, Ebola outbreaks, and Middle East tensions, humans still pointed the largest rocket ever built at the sky and lit the fuse — and it flew. Starship V3's successful test flight is a genuine milestone: NASA's own administrator confirmed it brings crewed lunar missions measurably closer to reality. When the world feels heavy, it helps to remember we are also, simultaneously, building the ships that will carry us to other worlds.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Starship V3 represents the most capable launch vehicle in human history completing a successful test, a prerequisite for returning humans to the Moon and eventual Mars missions.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Iran and the US are signaling progress toward a peace deal, with oil prices already falling on the news — a fragile but real diplomatic signal amid active conflict.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Fifteen years after the Joplin tornado, researchers document how the disaster drew nearly 100,000 volunteers and seeded a lasting culture of community kindness that persists today.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Soaring solar output and a surge in hydropower are pushing coal further off the US grid, with early 2026 data suggesting last year's slowdown in clean energy gains was an anomaly.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: An experimental mRNA vaccine shows promise protecting against multiple Ebola strains in rodent trials — potentially a game-changer for the very outbreak making headlines today.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Wide aerial view looking down at a 45-degree angle, vast flat Texas scrubland and Gulf Coast wetlands seen from high above, the landscape saturated in rich gold afternoon light filtering through sparse coastal vegetation, emerald marsh grasses and tidal flats glowing vivid green against amber earth. Tiny human figures — dozens of them — scattered across the scrubland below, all facing the same direction, faces invisible from this height, arms raised holding phones skyward toward something off-frame, their shadows stretching long and golden across the flat terrain. A narrow coastal road is clogged with parked cars stretching to the horizon, doors flung open, abandoned mid-journey by people who simply stopped and got out to watch. A cluster of pickup trucks sits on a sandy ridge overlooking the Gulf, their beds filled with more tiny silhouettes. The Gulf of Mexico shimmers emerald and gold in the distance, flat and enormous, the horizon vast and unobstructed. The entire scene communicates collective awe and interruption — ordinary life paused, a world momentarily stopped, all attention pulled toward the same invisible point in the sky above.
